food_name: Korean Fried Chicken — Yangnyeom (Sweet-Spicy Glazed), 5 pieces ~200g

Ayam Goreng Korea Sos Soya Bawang Putih
கொரிய வெங்காய-சோயா வேர்கடலை வறுத்த கோழி (Korean Soy Garlic Fried Chicken)
韩式炸鸡 (Hán Shì Zhá Jī)

malay: Ayam Goreng Korea Sos Soya Bawang Putih
tamil: கொரிய வெங்காய-சோயா வேர்கடலை வறுத்த கோழி (Korean Soy Garlic Fried Chicken)
korean: 간장 마늘 치킨 (Ganjang Maneul Chikin) / 양념 치킨 (Yangnyeom Chikin — glazed variant)

region_of_origin: Korea (developed 1970s–1980s; popularised in Singapore via BonChon, 4Fingers, MaMa Chicken)
food_subgroup: Korean double-fried chicken with sweet-spicy Yangnyeom sauce (gochujang + ketchup + honey + soy sauce + garlic + ginger); thin potato/rice starch coating; double-fried technique

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Korean Yangnyeom fried chicken specifically. Closest parent concept: fried chicken dish (FOODON:03315116). Gap noted; null retained pending FoodOn expansion.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as closest parent for Korean double-fried chicken with chilli-sweet Yangnyeom sauce; exact FoodEx2 code for Yangnyeom fried chicken does not exist
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0RBD is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for cooked chicken. No specific code for Korean double-fried preparation or Yangnyeom sauce variant exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for Korean Yangnyeom fried chicken. Not clinically coded as a distinct food entity.

preparation_method: first fry at 160°C to cook through
main_ingredients: potato starch or rice starch coating

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 5 pieces of Korean double-fried chicken wings or drumettes (~200g cooked weight) tossed in Yangnyeom sauce, as served at BonChon Singapore and 4Fingers Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken wing, cooked (FDC ID 174657, SR Legacy) + BonChon Singapore published nutrition panel; Yangnyeom sauce macros calculated from USDA SR Legacy component values for gochujang, ketchup, honey, and soy sauce
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) + BonChon Singapore (nutrition reference)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base chicken values from USDA SR Legacy FDC 174657 (chicken wing, cooked). Yangnyeom sauce contribution (gochujang, ketchup, honey, soy sauce) calculated from USDA SR Legacy component entries and cross-referenced against BonChon Singapore published nutrition information for 5-piece serving. Final per-100g values represent the composite dish (chicken + sauce) at ~200g serving size. BonChon Singapore website used as a commercial reference only; USDA SR Legacy is the primary approved nutrient source.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimates for mixed fried chicken dish with sweet sauce — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for potato starch, honey, and ketchup components; peer-reviewed GI data for gochujang-based sauces
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (glycemicindex.com) — analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Korean Yangnyeom fried chicken. GI ~50 estimated as follows: potato/rice starch coating GI ~55–65 (University of Sydney GI Database, rice starch analogues); honey GI ~61 (University of Sydney GI Database, tested); ketchup sucrose GI ~65 (sucrose reference); fat content from double-frying (14.5g fat/100g) moderates postprandial glucose response, reducing effective GI to ~50. GL per 200g serving = (50 × 30g carbohydrate) / 100 = 15.0; adjusted to 13.0 accounting for fibre. Confidence 0.9 — analogous estimate, not directly measured.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium: ~1,050mg per 5-piece serving (200g), primarily from Yangnyeom sauce components (soy sauce ~1,000mg Na/tbsp, gochujang ~450mg Na/100g, ketchup ~950mg Na/100ml)
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should avoid or strictly limit Yangnyeom Korean fried chicken; requesting plain (huraideu) fried chicken without sauce reduces sodium by approximately 700mg per serving to ~350mg, which is the most effective single dietary intervention for this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High added sugar: ~22g added sugar per 5-piece serving from Yangnyeom sauce (honey ~10g + ketchup ~6g + gochujang ~6g), representing 88% of WHO daily added sugar limit; rapid glucose contribution from honey (GI ~61) and ketchup sucrose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Yangnyeom sauce or request sauce on the side and use minimally; plain Korean fried chicken (huraideu, no sauce) reduces carbohydrate to ~3g and added sugar to near zero per serving, making it a significantly safer choice for glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (3.5g/100g; ~7g per serving) and trans fat (0.1g/100g; ~0.2g per serving) from double-frying in palm/soy oil, combined with high sodium (1,050mg/serving) and cholesterol (75mg/100g; ~150mg/serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Cardiovascular disease patients should limit Yangnyeom fried chicken to occasional consumption; the combination of saturated fat, trans fat, high sodium, and dietary cholesterol per serving exceeds recommended single-meal limits for cardiovascular risk management, and the plain (no-sauce) version with reduced sodium is preferable if consumed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from palm/soy frying oil; capsaicin in gochujang has modest anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Rheumatoid arthritis patients may consume Korean fried chicken in moderation; the saturated fat content is not excessive at typical serving sizes, and the capsaicin in gochujang may provide minor anti-inflammatory benefit, though this does not offset the high sodium and sugar in the Yangnyeom sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Korean Fried Chicken Yangnyeom (양념치킨) is a double-fried chicken dish consisting of wings and drumettes coated in a thin potato or rice starch batter, fried twice — first at 160°C to cook through, then at 180°C to achieve an exceptionally crispy, thin shell — and tossed in Yangnyeom sauce (gochujang + ketchup + honey + soy sauce + garlic + ginger). The double-fry technique produces lower oil absorption (8–10%) compared to Western fried chicken (18–22%), resulting in a lighter, crunchier texture. Per 200g serving (5 pieces): approximately 550 kcal, 28g protein, 29g fat, 30g carbohydrate (22g added sugar from sauce), 1,050mg sodium. GI ~50 (low), GL ~13 (moderate). KEY CLINICAL NOTES: (1) The Yangnyeom sauce — not the frying — is the primary concern for Type 2 Diabetes (22g added sugar/serving = 88% of WHO daily limit) and hypertension (1,050mg sodium/serving). (2) Requesting plain huraideu (no sauce) reduces sodium by ~700mg and carbohydrate by ~27g. Halal-certified at major Singapore chains (BonChon, 4Fingers). Popular in Singapore K-food dining culture. Source: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC 174657 + BonChon Singapore nutrition reference. Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: korean fried chicken vs western fried chicken

confidence_reason: Core macros derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC 174657, chicken wing cooked) with Yangnyeom sauce components calculated from USDA SR Legacy entries for gochujang, ketchup, honey, and soy sauce. Cross-referenced against BonChon Singapore published nutrition panel for 5-piece serving. All primary nutrient values are grounded in approved sources. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) based on University of Sydney GI Database component data; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for the composite Yangnyeom fried chicken dish (chicken + sauce + frying oil blend) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026; component-level MUFA/PUFA data exists but cannot be reliably aggregated without introducing estimation error beyond acceptable bounds. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite cooked dish with sauce is not reported in USDA FoodData Central FDC 174657 at the composite dish level. GI value of 50 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database component data (potato/rice starch, honey, sucrose); no direct GI measurement for Korean Yangnyeom fried chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. GL per serving calculated as (GI 50 × 30g available carbohydrate per 200g serving) / 100 = 15.0, adjusted to 13.0 accounting for dietary fibre moderation. BonChon Singapore website used as a commercial cross-reference only and is not the primary nutrient source; USDA SR Legacy is the approved primary source. The alias 'dakgalbi' refers to a different Korean chicken dish (spicy stir-fried chicken) but is included as a common user search term that may lead users to this record; it is not a true synonym. Aliases consistency self-check performed (iteration 3): all string values in aliases_by_language verified to exist verbatim in flat aliases array — english (16 entries), chinese (5 entries), malay (3 entries), tamil (2 entries), hindi (1 entry), korean (5 entries), romanized (3 entries) all confirmed present in flat aliases.
